replies.author: @DMac-YTuber
replies.text: 300M is about right.  The NET cost (gross expenditures minus UK film incentive) is about 300M, inclusive of reshoots.  The marketing and ancilliary distribution costs will amount to about 125M.  That’s a cost of roughly 425M net.  They’ll make around 200M in box office gross, which yields 100M in Disney profit after theaters take their 49% direct distributor cut.  Merchandise, pay-per-view streams, DVD, blu-ray, and soundtrack revenues will equate to another approximate 25M total in additional profits (which would normally be much higher for an actual successful movie…meaning this movie will underperform in domestic box office, international box office, AS WELL AS tertiary markets).  So Disney will net around 125M in combined revenue against a film that costs 425M…making it a 300M loss.

replies.author: @aquaham3564
replies.text: @DMac-YTuber  the cinemas take half, and then there is marketing. So for a movie to break even, the rule is rimes 2.5. This movie needed 700 million to break even. It is a 500 million loss.

replies.author: @DMac-YTuber
replies.text:
@aquaham3564 The reason they need to make 700M in the box office is NOT because the film costs 700M.  It’s because the box office receipts (700M needed) represent the GROSS sales BEFORE the theaters take their roughly 49% cut.  That money goes to the cineplexes and other places that need to make money by displaying the film (like, for example, Regal Theaters).  So if the box office receipts are 700M only about $350M actually goes to Disney.

This doesn’t mean the 700M is accurate.  But Disney pushes the proverbial needle on their revenues.  Don’t forget, after domestic box office revenues, and after international box office revenues, there is a tertiary avenue of revenue…which includes merchandise, DVD’s, blu-rays, soundtracks, and streaming purchases.  Disney is probably over-estimating how much they’ll make in the tertiary markets, driving the box office estimate down deceptively.  My belief is that this movie will under-perform in all three revenue avenues.  A better box office estimate should have been 800M…whereby Disney would get 400M of that, after theaters take their cut.  But, what’s undeniable—regardless of how you shift estimates across the three areas—is that Disney will lose big on this.

replies.author: @DMac-YTuber
replies.text:
@aquaham3564 You’re not doing the math correctly.  By your own admission you are saying it cost Disney $280M in production cost…

280M x 2.5 = 700M

So if the box office take is 700M then Disney gets 350M of it…280M of that covers the break even for production cost and 70M covers the marketing.  Again, that’s only a total of 350M in production AND marketing combined.  350M is far LESS than 500M.  They can’t lose more money than they spent.

What you failed to do in your math, when you reduced the 700M by their 200M actuals, you forgot to divide that balance of 500M by 2…because it was baked into the formula that the cinemas get half of that 500M.  Do the math again and you’ll see it is NOT a 500M loss.  It is only a 250M loss (half).  You can’t count the other half of the money, because it’s at the detriment of the cinema companies’ aggregates—who probably are not losing that much except by not shifting those screens to more profitable films.

That stated, I still say that the number is a 300M loss, not a 250M loss, for the reasons I already stated.

replies.author: @aquaham3564
replies.text: @DMac-YTuber  280? No I'm saying it cost them 350 million after reshoots.

replies.author: @DMac-YTuber
replies.text:
@aquaham3564 Even if that were the case (350M for production), the combined costs would not be too much higher.  The marketing would not have gone up in cost due to reshoots (we know this from the extended intermittent periods of “radio silence” in terms of publicity during the 2023 and 2024 period).  So marketing would have been roughly 100-125M, making the combined expenditure only $450-475M…still less than 500M.  And they’ve already recouped almost 100M of it.

I disagree, though, with a production cost going that high with reshoots.  As I understand it, most of the reshoots were to reinstate plot elements from the 1937 original that had been written out of the 2025 version.  No major set rebuilds would have been necessary.  Much of it was handled by CGI anyway; but the CGI was always going to be as extensive…which means no extra money was spent on post production onlining.  The cast were all Schedule F; so they would have been contractually obligated to complete the reshoots at no additional salary.  Basically, the only money needed for the reshoots would have been for stage lease, utilities, rewrite fee, props, below-the-line crew, and an extension on post production crew.  Assuming they were able to drop crew after each reshoot, it is unlikely that their net cost for the reshoots would have been such a huge cost, especially since they get the UK incentive off the gross expenditures.  The sweet thing about reshoots is that you generally don’t need to build sets, cast new characters, or carry weekly-hire crew, making reshoots much cheaper than principal photography, especially if no stunts or practical EFX are needed.  Many people tend to exaggerate the cost of reshoots.  I’ve worked on projects where reshoots cost less than 500k per shoot—full disclosure, those were tv and digital series; but the costs for theatrical features doesn’t necessarily run much higher.  It depends on WHAT is being reshot.

Bottom line, that Disney loss is still unlikely to exceed $300M by much.  

As for the cinemas, as their costs are amortized across all screens—and they can challenge the individual studios on the reducing or expanding of the numbers of screens—so they have been able to mitigate the Snow White disaster much better.  The theaters will most likely not lose any money on this dud.  They’ll just see a dip in their profits.  Had Minecraft not been a hit, the Snow White impact would have been much more severe on the theaters.  But, as it turned out, Minecraft saved the theaters from Snow White.  Disney—and the audiences who watched Snow White 2025–will be the only ones to lose money on this bad movie.

author: @dichotomus3764
text:
The most pernicious impact that these bad projects have is it injects discord into the fandom. I read lots of fan fiction as a kid. But if you make a bad addition to the cannon, suddenly those elements enter into the works of fans and new fans particularly. But the majority of fans like the work prior to the retcon, so there is no common starting point anymore. And fan engagement with the story (the lifeblood of the IP's value) dies. 

Example: Harry Potter during the first 7 books had hundreds of thousands of fan fictions written about it. This activity is what keeps fans eager for the next big thing. But if you change something in a later addition that retroactively changes all the prior works and dissolves the common starting point: unofficial fan engagement dies. Harry Potter and the cursed child made Hermione black. Just that little change disrupted canon to the point that fan engagement stagnated. And that was a play that relatively few watched. HBO's changes will have a farther reach. It will be the deathblow.

author: @Showman_87
text: The movie itself really isn’t bad, I actually found it enjoyable and not as woke as predicted. Admittedly it is flawed but it still has quite a few qualities I look for in Disney movies and it was a nice retelling of the Snow White story. Although I don’t agree with many of Rachel’s statements or her political views, I also don’t agree with many YouTubers I used to enjoy following for how much hate they’ve been building towards this movie in the last three years before it even released. I remember you saying you walked out of the movie because it was that bad but I don’t think you gave it a fair chance. It’s true that there has been toxicity in many Hollywood/Disney movies that has put off audiences but I’m realizing that YouTube comes with its own toxicity. It seems many YouTubers, especially on the conservative side, are locked into the anti-Disney/anti-woke mindset that they too can have skewed perceptions of what they see and are quick to react negatively to something just as much as those on the left side. I agree that many recent Disney movies haven’t been great and it has been disappointing for lifelong Disney fans, however there have been some good movies that weren’t given more credit because the instant negativity at the slightest hint of anything that might be perceived as woke automatically writes off any value the movie might have. I first noticed this when Elemental came out, I found it to be an incredible film but all YouTubers were focusing on was the supposed non-binary character so they rooted for it to fail just based on that. If you look for something to hate you’re going to find it, but if you have an open mind it’s more possible to see the beauty in something. There is value in being critical and having high standards but there’s also value in trying to see the good in something instead of immediately jumping on the negative, a testament that the classic Snow White represents. Although I have lost trust in Disney for the route they’ve taken I have also now lost trust in several YouTube creators because I don’t like my emotions being played with. The reception to this movie has taught me a great deal, although I’m disappointed that I can no longer invest my time in YouTubers such as yourself I do appreciate the work you’ve done and the part you played in teaching me to be wary of the online content I consume

Which shows that they didn't actually SEE the movie, because the prince doesn't rescue Snow White at all. If giving a true love kiss of goodbye (because he thought she was dead, like the dwarfs did), is rescuing, only then could you say he did. But at least Gadot knew enough to shut up. Zegler, poured fuel on the fire, every time she opened her mouth. So, that the supposed "apology" tour never had the ring of truth to it. She got what she ASKED for. She said if you don't agree with her, she doesn't need your business. So, she didn't get much business.
